verbatim: The Board must set the tone-at-the-top on the importance of safeguarding customer information and the potential consequences on the FSP in the event of a customer information breach. The Board shall also exercise its oversight function in all matters pertaining to the proper handling of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: The board of directors must champion customer information protection from the top, setting clear expectations and actively overseeing all aspects of how the FSP handles customer data.

verbatim: The Board must approve the FSP's written policies and ensure that written policies, procedures and controls are in place to provide adequate protection over the confidentiality and security of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: The board must formally approve information protection policies and verify that comprehensive written policies, procedures, and controls exist to adequately safeguard customer data confidentiality and security.

verbatim: The Board must oversee the implementation and maintenance of the policies and procedures, including reviewing reports relating to the management of customer information from senior management. These reports should include risk assessments, findings, and mitigation plans. The Board must be satisfied that the policies, procedures and controls are adequate and effective in safeguarding customer information. These reports and mitigation plans must be periodically reviewed to reflect changes in the operating environment and evolving risks, ensuring continued relevance and effectiveness.
translation: AI Generated: The board must actively monitor policy implementation by reviewing senior management reports on customer information management, including risk assessments and mitigation plans. The board must confirm adequacy and effectiveness of controls, with periodic reviews to adapt to changing risks.

verbatim: The Board must require written assurance from senior management annually that the controls in place to protect customer information are working effectively and the FSP's outsourced service providers fulfil their obligations in accordance with the contract provisions on safeguarding customer information.
translation: AI Generated: The board must obtain annual written assurance from senior management confirming that customer information protection controls are effective and that outsourced service providers are meeting their contractual obligations.

verbatim: Senior management must be responsible and accountable for establishing and implementing procedures including effective systems and controls to safeguard customer information.
translation: AI Generated: Senior management bears direct responsibility and accountability for building and running effective systems and controls that protect customer information.

verbatim: Senior management must also designate a person of sufficient senior ranking with the overall responsibility for the implementation and on-going maintenance of policies, procedures and controls with regard to safeguarding customer information. The responsibilities of the designated officer must include, but are not limited to- (a) communicating relevant policies throughout the FSP to ensure consistent implementation of processes and procedures; and (b) coordinating with key stakeholders within the FSP to comply with this Policy Document.
translation: AI Generated: Senior management must appoint a senior officer responsible for ongoing implementation and maintenance of customer information protection, including policy communication and stakeholder coordination.

verbatim: FSPs may consider establishing or designating an existing position such as the chief data officer, chief information officer or data protection officer to carry out the responsibilities in paragraph 9.2.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs are encouraged to assign the designated officer role to existing positions like CDO, CIO, or DPO rather than necessarily creating a new role.

verbatim: Senior management must also place the responsibility on the business and functional lines of the FSP in preserving the confidentiality and security of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: Responsibility for customer data confidentiality and security must be embedded across all business and functional lines, not just centralised in one team.

verbatim: Senior management must ensure that the FSP's appointed representatives and agents also have in place appropriate and adequate control measures to properly safeguard customer information.
translation: AI Generated: Representatives and agents of the FSP must also maintain adequate controls to protect customer information.

verbatim: Senior management must communicate a clear message to staff and the FSP's appointed representatives and agents, on the importance of preserving the confidentiality and security of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: Senior management must clearly communicate to all staff, representatives, and agents about the critical importance of protecting customer information confidentiality.

verbatim: Senior management must also ensure that adequate training on relevant policies is provided to staff and that the appointed representatives and agents provide adequate training to their staff.
translation: AI Generated: Adequate training on information protection policies must be provided to all staff, and representatives/agents must similarly train their own staff.

verbatim: Senior management must ensure that an independent review is carried out at least once in every two years in accordance with paragraphs 10.53, 10.54, 10.55 and 10.56 on the effectiveness of policies, procedures and control measures in protecting customer information.
translation: AI Generated: An independent review of the effectiveness of customer information protection policies, procedures, and controls must be conducted at least every two years.

verbatim: Senior management must notify the Board upon the detection of customer information breaches, depending on the nature of the breach and sensitivity of the customer information.
translation: AI Generated: Senior management must promptly notify the board when customer information breaches are detected, with urgency proportional to breach severity and data sensitivity.

verbatim: Senior management must also report to the Board on the findings of the investigation of customer information breaches, in accordance with paragraph 11.21.
translation: AI Generated: Senior management must report breach investigation findings to the board as required under the investigation reporting requirements.

verbatim: FSPs must identify and address potential threats and vulnerabilities that could result in theft, loss, misuse, or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means, of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs are required to proactively identify and remediate threats and vulnerabilities that may lead to any form of compromise of customer information, whether through theft, loss, misuse, or unauthorised access, modification, or disclosure.

verbatim: FSPs must also assess the likelihood that identified threats and vulnerabilities will materialise, and the potential impact on both the FSP and its customers in the event a customer information breach occurs. This assessment, among other considerations, must include third-party service providers and outsourcing arrangements, and be supported by mitigation plans that are developed and implemented based on the threats identified.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must perform a likelihood-and-impact assessment of identified threats and vulnerabilities, covering third-party and outsourcing risks, and must develop and implement corresponding mitigation plans.

verbatim: Threats and vulnerabilities to customer information can be internal or external and could be due to negligence or a deliberate act of any person.
translation: AI Generated: Threats and vulnerabilities may originate from internal or external sources and may arise from either negligent or intentional conduct.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure the risk assessment referred to under paragraph 10.2 is proportionate to the size, nature and complexity of the FSP's operations as well as the amount and sensitivity of customer information held.
translation: AI Generated: The risk assessment must be scaled proportionately to the FSP's operational size, nature, complexity, and the volume and sensitivity of customer information it holds.

verbatim: FSPs may leverage on the existing arrangements, functions or tools that have a similar focus on managing risks to the confidentiality and security of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs are encouraged to reuse existing risk management arrangements, functions, or tools that already address confidentiality and security of customer information, rather than building entirely new frameworks.

verbatim: FSPs must establish and have in place written policies and procedures to safeguard customer information, and this must include the collection, access, storage, use, transmission, sharing, disclosure and disposal of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must have documented policies and procedures covering the full lifecycle of customer information: collection, access, storage, use, transmission, sharing, disclosure, and disposal.

footnotes.text: Policies refer to documented principles that express a FSP's goals and objectives and determine the formulation of strategy, plans, actions and procedures.
Procedures refer to detailed steps to be followed as a consistent approach to put into action the policies approved by the Board in day-to-day operations.

verbatim: The written policies and procedures must be proportionate to the FSP's size, nature and complexity of the FSP's operations and the amount and sensitivity of customer information the FSP handles.
translation: AI Generated: Policies and procedures must be proportionate to the FSP's size, operational complexity, and the volume and sensitivity of customer information it handles.

verbatim: Without limiting the generality of paragraphs 10.6 and 10.7, FSPs must have clear written policies and procedures governing these areas- (a) off-site work arrangements that allow access to customer information in the FSP's systems; (b) handling and transporting of physical documents containing customer information outside the FSP's premises; (c) the use of portable IT equipment and data storage devices; and (d) customer information breach incident handling.
translation: AI Generated: At a minimum, FSPs must have specific written policies and procedures for: (a) remote/off-site work access, (b) physical document handling and transport, (c) portable IT equipment and storage devices, and (d) breach incident handling.

verbatim: FSPs may incorporate the requirements on the proper handling of customer information in other policies, if appropriate. These may include written policies on human resource, code of conduct, information security, outsourcing and the disclosure of customer information to parties permitted under the law.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs may integrate customer information handling requirements into existing policies such as HR, code of conduct, information security, outsourcing, and lawful disclosure policies.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that the written policies and procedures are readily accessible and clearly communicated to staff by the person designated under paragraph 9.2, to ensure compliance with such policies and procedures.
translation: AI Generated: The designated officer under paragraph 9.2 must ensure that policies and procedures are easily accessible and clearly communicated to all staff to drive compliance.

verbatim: FSPs must continually review their written policies and procedures to ensure that they remain adequate, relevant and operate effectively in response to changes in the operating environment.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must continuously review and update their policies and procedures to ensure they remain adequate, relevant, and effective as the operating environment evolves.

verbatim: FSPs must deploy preventive and detective ICT controls to- (a) prevent theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure of customer information; and (b) detect errors and irregularities when they occur.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must implement both preventive and detective ICT controls to stop and detect theft, loss, misuse, unauthorised access, modification, disclosure, errors, and irregularities involving customer information.

verbatim: FSPs must regularly monitor the effectiveness of these controls to ensure that they remain responsive to changing threats.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must conduct regular monitoring of their ICT controls to ensure they remain effective and responsive to evolving threats.

verbatim: On occasions where FSPs' staff, representatives and agents undertake offsite work arrangements, FSPs must have in place appropriate controls for such offsite work arrangements including for the ICT equipment used that allow access into FSPs' systems and customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must establish appropriate controls for off-site work arrangements, including controls over ICT equipment used by staff, representatives, and agents to access FSP systems and customer information remotely.

verbatim: The controls referred to in paragraph 10.14 may include robust authentication for remote access into FSPs' systems, encrypting data stored on the ICT equipment and ensuring data transmission is securely protected.
translation: AI Generated: Examples of off-site work controls include strong authentication for remote access, encryption of data stored on ICT equipment, and secure data transmission.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that only staff with a legitimate business need is allowed to download customer information into portable storage devices provided by the FSP.
translation: AI Generated: Only staff with a legitimate business need may download customer information to FSP-provided portable storage devices.

verbatim: FSPs must put in place the relevant controls to ensure that the customer information stored in such portable storage devices pursuant to paragraph 10.16 is adequately protected, such as password and data encryption, to prevent theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means.
translation: AI Generated: Customer information stored on portable storage devices must be protected with controls such as password protection and data encryption to prevent any form of compromise.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that staff are given access to call recordings on a strictly "need-to-know" basis for recorded telephone conversations with customers that contain customer information.
translation: AI Generated: Access to call recordings containing customer information must be restricted to staff on a strict need-to-know basis.

verbatim: FSPs may consider disabling USB ports and CD writers on desktop and laptop computers of staff who do not have any operational need to download, transmit or store customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs are encouraged to disable USB ports and CD writers on computers of staff who do not operationally need to download, transmit, or store customer information.

verbatim: FSPs must have in place mechanisms that create a strong deterrent effect against unauthorised disclosure by whatever means of customer information by staff.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must implement mechanisms that strongly deter staff from making unauthorised disclosures of customer information by any means.

verbatim: Unauthorised disclosure may occur in many ways and forms such as staff taking photograph of documents or screens that contain customer information. The mechanisms referred to in paragraph 10.20 may include raising staff awareness on the disciplinary actions for unauthorised disclosure by whatever means, installing CCTV at relevant areas, having an open office concept, encouraging whistleblowing in this respect, or restricting personal electronic devices at high risk areas like data centres, dealing rooms, call centres, etc.
translation: AI Generated: Deterrent mechanisms may include staff awareness of disciplinary actions, CCTV installation, open office layouts, whistleblowing encouragement, and restricting personal electronic devices in high-risk areas such as data centres, dealing rooms, and call centres.

verbatim: FSPs must restrict access to web-based communication websites and social media platforms, particularly those which are encrypted from end-to-end (e.g., WhatsApp Desktop, Facebook and Skype Messenger) on staff who handle customer information, to prevent unauthorised disclosure of customer information to external parties via internet services.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must restrict staff who handle customer information from accessing web-based communication and social media platforms, especially end-to-end encrypted ones like WhatsApp Desktop, Facebook, and Skype Messenger, to prevent unauthorised external disclosure.

verbatim: FSPs must also implement mechanisms for the prompt detection of- (a) unauthorised access to customer information; (b) unusual frequent viewing of customer information in the FSPs' systems by staff; (c) unusual or suspicious downloading activities that involve customer information; and (d) unauthorised disclosure of customer information to external parties.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must implement detection mechanisms for: (a) unauthorised access, (b) unusual frequent viewing by staff, (c) suspicious downloading activities, and (d) unauthorised disclosure of customer information to external parties.

verbatim: The mechanisms referred to in paragraph 10.23 may include installing key-logger software, conducting regular reviews of audit trail and carrying out random periodic sample checks.
translation: AI Generated: Detection mechanisms may include key-logger software, regular audit trail reviews, and random periodic sample checks.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that the role profile for each type of job includes a description of the access rights to customer information if relevant, for staff to perform their jobs in accordance with the access rights.
translation: AI Generated: Each job role profile must include a description of the relevant access rights to customer information, ensuring staff operate within defined access boundaries.

verbatim: FSPs must identify the location of customer information residing in different systems and ensure that adequate access controls are in place at different levels (i.e. application level, database level, operating system level and network level) to prevent unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means of customer information to external parties.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must map where customer information resides across systems and implement access controls at application, database, operating system, and network levels to prevent unauthorised access, modification, or disclosure.

verbatim: FSPs must regularly review the access rights of staff and immediately revoke the access rights of any staff leaving the FSP or changing to a new role or position that does not require access to customer information to prevent the theft of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must regularly review staff access rights and immediately revoke access when staff leave the organisation or move to roles that no longer require access to customer information.

verbatim: FSPs must implement adequate physical security controls to ensure customer information stored either in paper or electronic forms are properly protected against theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must implement physical security controls to protect customer information in both paper and electronic forms against all forms of compromise.

verbatim: FSPs must restrict access and implement robust intruder deterrents in areas where large amounts, or critical and sensitive customer information are accessible and stored, for example, the server and filing rooms.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must restrict access and deploy intruder deterrents in areas storing large volumes of or critical and sensitive customer information, such as server rooms and filing rooms.

verbatim: To minimise the risks of theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means of customer information, FSPs may consider implementing a clear-desk policy.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs are encouraged to implement a clear-desk policy to reduce the risk of customer information being compromised through physical exposure.

subsection: C: Control measures - Off-site and data-in-transit
verbatim: FSPs must provide clear policy and procedures encompassing adequate controls to be put in place for the proper handling of customer information collected off-site and in-transit. This must include ensuring that physical documents are securely stored while the customer information stored in portable devices are securely protected to prevent theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must have clear policies and procedures with adequate controls for handling customer information collected off-site and in-transit, ensuring secure storage of physical documents and protection of information on portable devices.

verbatim: To effectively safeguard customer information throughout its lifecycle, FSPs must have proper procedures in place to identify customer information that is no longer required from the perspective of the operation or requirements of any written law. FSPs shall deploy appropriate methods to securely dispose of such customer information which includes any paper and digital records of the customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must have procedures to identify customer information no longer needed operationally or legally, and must deploy appropriate methods to securely dispose of both paper and digital records.

verbatim: Customer information is considered securely disposed of when it is beyond any possibility of recovery, is irreversible or cannot be reconstructed in any way.
translation: AI Generated: Secure disposal means customer information is irrecoverable, irreversible, and cannot be reconstructed by any means.

verbatim: To ensure secure data destruction, all devices may undergo proper sanitization using industry standard method and chain of custody controls, such as data wiping software, physical destruction or degaussing. For information stored in digital devices, a simple file deletion or reformatting of hard drives and portable storage devices may not be sufficient to completely destroy the stored information.
translation: AI Generated: Devices should be sanitised using industry-standard methods such as data wiping software, physical destruction, or degaussing, with chain of custody controls. Simple file deletion or reformatting is insufficient for digital devices.

verbatim: FSPs must assess the risks and benefits of engaging an outsourced service provider for the destruction of customer information which involves transporting customer information outside the FSP's premises.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must conduct a risk-benefit assessment before engaging an outsourced service provider for customer information destruction, particularly where it involves transporting information off-premises.

verbatim: FSPs must shred or store customer information in a manner that is inaccessible such as sealed in bags with tamper proof fasteners or stored in locked containers before it is collected by outsourced service providers for destruction.
translation: AI Generated: Before collection by outsourced service providers, customer information must be shredded or stored inaccessibly in tamper-proof sealed bags or locked containers.

verbatim: FSPs must conduct random checks on the collection and destruction process carried out by outsourced service providers to ensure that customer information is properly destroyed.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must perform random checks on outsourced service providers' collection and destruction processes to verify proper destruction of customer information.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: Human factors are common contributory causes to theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means of customer information. It is therefore important that all staff understand the importance of protecting the confidentiality and security of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: Human factors are a common cause of customer information compromise, making it essential that all staff understand the importance of protecting customer information confidentiality and security.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs must ensure that employment contracts contain a provision requiring all staff to sign a confidentiality undertaking that clearly specifies the obligation and requirement under the relevant written law to safeguard customer information as well as the consequences for failure to comply with such obligation and requirement.
translation: AI Generated: Employment contracts must include a confidentiality undertaking signed by all staff, specifying legal obligations to safeguard customer information and consequences for non-compliance.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: Where FSPs engage with external vendors to carry out duties or services within the FSPs' premises (e.g., security guards, cleaners and maintenance officers/engineers), FSPs must ensure that the external vendors carry out an appropriate level of vetting and monitoring on their personnel to reduce the risk of customer information theft.
translation: AI Generated: When engaging external vendors working on-premises (e.g., security guards, cleaners, maintenance staff), FSPs must ensure vendors appropriately vet and monitor their personnel to mitigate customer information theft risk.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs must ensure a high degree of staff awareness at all times on the following: (a) the need to protect the confidentiality and security of customer information; (b) the importance of complying with relevant policies and procedures established by the FSPs; and (c) the consequences if any staff is involved in any theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must maintain a high level of ongoing staff awareness on: (a) protecting customer information confidentiality and security, (b) complying with relevant policies and procedures, and (c) the consequences of involvement in any customer information breach.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs must have in place robust monitoring mechanisms to ensure that the relevant policies, procedures and controls established by the FSPs are adhered to by staff.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must implement robust monitoring mechanisms to ensure staff adhere to established policies, procedures, and controls.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs must provide relevant training and regularly remind all staff on their obligations to ensure the confidentiality of customer information is safeguarded when dealing with such information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must provide training and regular reminders to all staff about their obligations to safeguard the confidentiality of customer information.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs must include in their programme for new staff, specific trainings to explain the relevant policies and procedures on protecting customer information.
translation: AI Generated: New staff onboarding programmes must include specific training on the FSP's policies and procedures for protecting customer information.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs must ensure that new staff are notified on the possible actions that may be taken for non-compliance with policies and procedures.
translation: AI Generated: New staff must be informed of the possible disciplinary or other actions that may result from non-compliance with customer information protection policies and procedures.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: Guidance provided to staff on safeguarding customer information should be concise and reader-friendly to enable understanding among staff on how to comply with the relevant policies and procedures.
translation: AI Generated: Staff guidance on safeguarding customer information should be concise and easy to understand to promote effective compliance.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs must have in place mechanisms to gauge the effectiveness of trainings to staff on safeguarding customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must have mechanisms to measure the effectiveness of staff training on safeguarding customer information.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: The mechanisms referred to in paragraph 10.47 may include conducting an annual awareness survey to assess the level of understanding among staff on protecting the confidentiality and security of customer information and reporting a customer information breach.
translation: AI Generated: Training effectiveness mechanisms may include annual awareness surveys to assess staff understanding of customer information protection and breach reporting.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs must conduct a thorough and timely investigation upon detecting theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means of customer information by staff and take appropriate actions against the staff concerned.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must promptly and thoroughly investigate any detected customer information breach by staff and take appropriate action against those involved.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: The actions taken pursuant to paragraph 10.49 must send a strong message to all staff and act as deterrent to prevent future recurrence of the customer information breach. Where the FSP decides not to take action against the staff in breach, the reason for not taking any action must be properly documented and approved by senior management.
translation: AI Generated: Disciplinary actions must serve as a strong deterrent to all staff. If the FSP chooses not to act against a staff member in breach, the rationale must be documented and approved by senior management.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: In accordance with paragraph 9.10, FSPs must report to the Board the result of the investigation and actions taken against the staff concerned.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must report investigation outcomes and actions taken against staff to the Board, in line with paragraph 9.10.

subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
verbatim: FSPs shall remain accountable for the conduct and actions of their appointed representatives and agents for any theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs retain full accountability for the conduct and actions of their appointed representatives and agents regarding any customer information breach.

verbatim: FSPs must subject their written policies, procedures and control measures for safeguarding customer information to an independent review at least once in every two years.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must have their customer information protection policies, procedures, and controls independently reviewed at least once every two years.

footnotes.text: Independent review is to be carried out by a function independent of the business units involved in the handling of customer information, such as internal audit. There is no expectation for FSPs to engage an external party to carry out the independent review.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that the independent review must include an assessment of the effectiveness of senior management and their oversight as well as the adequacy and effectiveness of measures undertaken by the FSP to protect customer information from theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means.
translation: AI Generated: The independent review must assess both senior management effectiveness and oversight, and the adequacy and effectiveness of measures to protect customer information.

verbatim: The independent reviewer under paragraph 10.53 must communicate the written findings to the senior management and the Board of the FSP.
translation: AI Generated: The independent reviewer must provide written findings to both senior management and the Board.

footnotes.text: The written findings should include the review's scope, methodologies used, findings, evidence obtained, and any limitations of the review

verbatim: Based on the written findings under paragraph 10.55, senior management must ensure that appropriate and timely actions are taken to rectify any deficiencies in the control measures.
translation: AI Generated: Senior management must take appropriate and timely corrective actions to address any deficiencies identified in the independent review findings.

verbatim: FSPs must have in place a customer information breach handling and response plan to address any theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means of customer information. For the avoidance of doubt, FSPs must ensure that their customer information breach handling and response plans comprise of deliberate and non-deliberate customer information breaches.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must establish a breach handling and response plan covering theft, loss, misuse, or unauthorised access, modification, or disclosure of customer information, encompassing both deliberate and non-deliberate breaches.

verbatim: The plan by FSPs under paragraph 11.1 must at a minimum, include escalation procedures and a clear line of responsibility to contain and take remedial actions relating to the customer information breach to prevent any future recurrence of the breach.
translation: AI Generated: The breach response plan must include, at minimum, escalation procedures and clear responsibility lines for containment, remediation, and prevention of recurrence.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that all staff understand the escalation procedures and that the relevant staff are trained to effectively take the appropriate remedial actions for a customer information breach to protect affected customers' interests.
translation: AI Generated: All staff must understand escalation procedures, and relevant staff must be trained to take effective remedial actions to protect affected customers during a breach.

verbatim: FSPs must have in place a mechanism to identify customer information breaches including those which arise from customer complaints and the FSPs must investigate the complaints promptly and properly.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must have mechanisms to detect breaches, including those surfaced through customer complaints, and must investigate such complaints promptly and properly.

verbatim: FSPs must take appropriate mitigating actions to contain a customer information breach immediately.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must act immediately to contain any customer information breach through appropriate mitigation measures.

verbatim: In the event that a customer information breach at one FSP involves customer data belonging to another FSP, the FSP in which the breach occurred must promptly notify the affected FSP to ensure that timely and appropriate remedial actions are undertaken.
translation: AI Generated: When a breach at one FSP involves customer data belonging to another FSP, the breached FSP must promptly notify the other FSP so timely remedial actions can be taken.

verbatim: FSPs must assess the impact arising from the theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means of customer information. In ascertaining the impact of the customer information breach, FSPs must have regard to, at a minimum, the following: (a) whether the breach involved accidental errors or intentional and malicious action; (b) the type and sensitivity of customer information involved; (c) the number of customers affected; (d) to whom the customer information was exposed to; and (e) the likelihood of the customer information being used for fraudulent or other harmful purposes.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must assess breach impact considering at minimum: whether it was accidental or intentional, the type and sensitivity of data, number of affected customers, who the data was exposed to, and likelihood of fraudulent or harmful use.

verbatim: FSPs must notify BNM immediately upon becoming aware of a customer information breach where the breach - (a) poses or is likely to pose reputational risk to the FSP or a threat to public confidence and trust; (b) causes or is likely to cause significant harm to affected customer(s); or (c) involves or is likely to involve a large number of customers (i.e. significant scale).
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must immediately notify BNM upon discovering a breach that poses reputational risk or threatens public confidence, causes or may cause significant harm to customers, or affects or may affect a large number of customers.

footnotes.text: In addition to notifying BNM, FSPs as data controllers shall comply with the mandatory data breach notification obligation to the Personal Data Protection Commissioner as prescribed in in Section 12B of PDPA and Circular of Personal Data Protection Commissioner No.2/2025 (Data Breach Notification), effective 1 June 2025 onwards.

verbatim: With respect to paragraph 11.8(a), a customer information breach poses or is likely to pose reputational risk to the FSP or a threat to public confidence and trust, where the breach involves, includes but not limited to where it fulfils any of the following circumstances: (a) disclosures to a party suspected of being involved in criminal activity; (b) information being made public or circulated via any medium including the social media; or (c) a customer known to the public, e.g., a celebrity or a public figure or where the breach is likely to attract media attention.
translation: AI Generated: A breach poses reputational risk or threatens public confidence when it involves disclosure to suspected criminals, public circulation including via social media, or concerns a public figure or is likely to attract media attention. This list is non-exhaustive.

footnotes.text: For the avoidance of doubt, this is a non-exhaustive list.

verbatim: With respect to paragraph 11.8(b), FSPs shall consider that a customer information breach causes or is likely to cause significant harm if there is a risk that the breach: (a) may result in financial loss, damage to or loss of property, loss of business opportunities, damage to reputation, a negative effect on credit record or threat to safety of customer(s); (b) may be misused for illegal purposes; (c) could enable identification of theft or fraud; or (d) consists of sensitive customer information.
translation: AI Generated: A breach causes significant harm if it risks financial loss, property damage, reputational harm, credit record impact, safety threats, illegal misuse, identity theft or fraud, or involves sensitive customer information.

footnotes.text: Examples of sensitive customer information include security credential to access customer's account, biometric data, medical or health related information.

verbatim: With respect to paragraph 11.8(c), FSPs shall consider that a customer information breach involves or is likely to involve a large number of customers (i.e. significant scale) if the number of affected customers exceed one thousand (1,000).
translation: AI Generated: A breach is of significant scale if the number of affected customers exceeds 1,000.

verbatim: In the event a customer information breach meets the criteria of significant scale under paragraph 11.11, FSPs must assess the potential impact and take appropriate actions to avoid or reduce any harm on the affected customers.
translation: AI Generated: When a breach reaches significant scale (over 1,000 customers), FSPs must assess the potential impact and take appropriate actions to avoid or reduce harm to affected customers.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that the assessment of significant harm is confirmed by the designated officer of sufficient senior ranking as specified under paragraph 9.2.
translation: AI Generated: The assessment of whether significant harm has occurred must be confirmed by the designated senior officer appointed under paragraph 9.2.

verbatim: With respect to customer information breaches that have been made public and circulated via any medium such as social media, FSPs must effectively manage the reputational risk arising from it, including ensuring immediate notification to BNM under paragraph 11.8.
translation: AI Generated: When breaches become public via social media or other channels, FSPs must manage the resulting reputational risk and ensure immediate BNM notification.

verbatim: If based on FSP's assessment a customer information breach appears to involve fraud, criminal activity or may result in identity theft, FSPs must also notify the relevant law enforcement agency, as soon as practicable.
translation: AI Generated: If a breach appears to involve fraud, criminal activity, or potential identity theft, FSPs must also notify the relevant law enforcement agency as soon as practicable.

verbatim: With respect to paragraph 11.8, the notification to BNM must, at a minimum, include the following information: (a) a description of the customer information breach that has occurred, including the type or nature of customer information that has been affected by the breach; (b) the number of affected customers; (c) consequences or harm to the affected customers due to the customer information breach; (d) potential consequences or harm to the affected customers that may arise as a result of the customer information breach; and (e) a description of measures that have been taken or will be taken by the FSP to address the breach.
translation: AI Generated: The BNM notification must include at minimum: a breach description with data types affected, number of affected customers, actual and potential consequences or harm, and measures taken or planned to address the breach.

verbatim: In the event the complete information as required under paragraph 11.16 is not available to be submitted to BNM as required, under paragraph 11.8, the FSP must adhere to the following: (a) the FSP shall proceed to immediately provide BNM with all other information that is available at the time; (b) on the information under paragraph 11.16(b), the FSP must submit the estimated number of the affected customers based on their initial assessment of the breach; and (c) the FSP must take timely and relevant measures to determine the final number of affected customers without undue delay with respect to paragraph 11.16(b). Once the complete information required under paragraph 11.16 is obtained, the FSP must provide this information to BNM immediately.
translation: AI Generated: If complete information is unavailable when notifying BNM, FSPs must immediately provide whatever information is available, submit estimated affected customer numbers based on initial assessment, and work without undue delay to determine final numbers. Complete information must be submitted to BNM as soon as obtained.

subsection: C: Submission of detailed investigation report
verbatim: FSPs must carry out an investigation to ascertain the root causes of a customer information breach and determine appropriate remedial actions to prevent future recurrence. Additionally, FSPs must ensure that the investigation must be carried out by a competent party, overseen by a party independent of the relevant business unit where the breach occurred.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must investigate breach root causes and determine remedial actions to prevent recurrence. The investigation must be conducted by a competent party and overseen by a party independent of the business unit where the breach occurred.

footnotes.text: Competent party refers to a party with the relevant expertise and experience in assessing a customer information breach.

subsection: C: Submission of detailed investigation report
verbatim: FSPs must complete the investigation within three (3) months upon detecting a customer information breach having regard to the complexity of the breach and table a detailed investigation report to the Board.
translation: AI Generated: Investigations must be completed within three months of detecting a breach, considering breach complexity, and a detailed investigation report must be tabled to the Board.

subsection: C: Submission of detailed investigation report
verbatim: FSPs must submit the detailed investigation report and Appendix 1 to BNM within one (1) working day upon tabling to the Board in respect of a customer information breach that- (a) causes or is likely to cause significant harm to the affected customer(s); (b) is of significant scale (i.e., affected customers exceeds or is likely to exceed 1,000); or (c) involves a deliberate attempt on unauthorised disclosure of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: The detailed investigation report and Appendix 1 must be submitted to BNM within one working day of Board tabling for breaches causing significant harm, exceeding 1,000 affected customers, or involving deliberate unauthorised disclosure.

footnotes.text: For the avoidance of doubt, the definition of significant harm as specified in paragraph 11.10(a) shall apply for the purpose of paragraph 11.20(a).

subsection: C: Submission of detailed investigation report
verbatim: With respect to paragraph 11.20, FSPs must ensure that the detailed investigation report to be submitted to BNM is signed off by the designated officer of sufficient senior ranking as specified under paragraph 9.2 and shall be submitted to- Director Consumer and Market Conduct Department Bank Negara Malaysia Jalan Dato' Onn 50480 Kuala Lumpur. For non-bank approved issuers of designated payment instruments and Islamic designated payment instruments as well as approved and registered operators of a payment system, the abovementioned report must be submitted to- Director Payment Services Oversight Department Bank Negara Malaysia Jalan Dato' Onn 50480 Kuala Lumpur
translation: AI Generated: The investigation report must be signed off by the designated senior officer under paragraph 9.2 and submitted to the Director of Consumer and Market Conduct Department at BNM. For non-bank payment instrument issuers and payment system operators, the report must be submitted to the Director of Payment Services Oversight Department at BNM.

subsection: C: Submission of detailed investigation report
verbatim: If a customer information breach at one FSP involves a customer information breach belonging to another FSP, it is the responsibility of the FSP in which the customer information breach occurred to carry out the required actions specified under paragraphs 11.18 to 11.21.
translation: AI Generated: When a breach at one FSP involves another FSP's customer data, the FSP where the breach occurred bears responsibility for carrying out all investigation and reporting actions under paragraphs 11.18 to 11.21.

subsection: C: Submission of detailed investigation report
verbatim: FSPs must put in place a register to record all customer information breaches covering the root causes, remedial actions and lessons learnt to prevent future recurrences.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must maintain a breach register recording all customer information breaches, their root causes, remedial actions taken, and lessons learnt to prevent recurrence.

subsection: C: Submission of detailed investigation report
verbatim: With respect to paragraph 11.23, FSP must retain the investigation reports of all customer information breach incidents for at least seven (7) years from the date the reports are tabled to the Board.
translation: AI Generated: Investigation reports for all breach incidents must be retained for at least seven years from the date they are tabled to the Board.

verbatim: Where the customer information breach meets the definition of causing, or is likely to cause, significant harm as outlined in paragraph 11.10, the FSP must notify the affected customers without undue delay after the notification is made to BNM under paragraph 11.8.
translation: AI Generated: When a breach causes or is likely to cause significant harm as defined in paragraph 11.10, the FSP must notify affected customers without undue delay after notifying BNM.

verbatim: With respect to paragraph 11.25, FSPs shall ensure any delay of the notification to the affected customers does not cause further harm to the affected customers.
translation: AI Generated: Any delay in notifying affected customers must not result in further harm to those customers.

verbatim: When notifying affected customers under paragraph 11.25, FSPs must ensure that the affected customers, at a minimum, are provided with the following information: (a) a brief description of the customer information breach that has occurred; (b) details of the potential consequences to the customer as a result of the breach; (c) advice on the steps that should be taken by the customer to reduce or mitigate any potential consequences resulting from the breach; (d) a description of the measures taken or proposed to be taken by the FSP to remedy the breach and mitigate its potential consequences; and (e) FSP's contact details from whom more information or assistance regarding the customer information breach can be obtained.
translation: AI Generated: Customer notifications must include at minimum: a breach description, potential consequences, advice on protective steps customers should take, FSP measures to remedy the breach, and FSP contact details for further information or assistance.

footnotes.text: For the avoidance of doubt, this includes advising the customer on any potential harm that may result from the breach.
Examples of protective measures include advising the customer to change their passwords or PINs, monitoring their accounts for suspicious activities and being vigilant against phishing attempts.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that notification to the affected customers is clear and written in plain language. The FSP must draw the customers' attention to the steps that they should take to protect themselves from any potential consequences in view of the customer information breach.
translation: AI Generated: Notifications must be clear and in plain language, drawing customers' attention to protective steps they should take against potential consequences of the breach.

verbatim: FSPs must notify the affected customers directly. However, in exceptional circumstances where direct notification would entail disproportionate effort, the FSP may issue a public announcement and display prominent notices at the FSP's branches and websites, provided such measures are sufficient.
translation: AI Generated: Affected customers must be notified directly. In exceptional cases where direct notification would require disproportionate effort, FSPs may instead issue public announcements and display prominent notices at branches and websites, if sufficient.

footnotes.text: Direct notification refers to notification made to each individual affected customer through any appropriate communication channels such as registered mail, email, phone call, short messaging service (SMS), push notification or in-app notification.

Disproportionate effort refers to the effort required to notify each individual customer directly that is significantly greater than what is reasonable or practical, considering the circumstances of the breach, the number of affected customers and the available resources of the FSP.
Public announcement refers to a statement or notice made available to the general public through appropriate channels such as press releases, social media or official website.

verbatim: For the avoidance of doubt, in the event an FSP is unable to identify the specific customer(s) who are affected by a customer information breach at the point it is required to notify the affected customer under paragraph 11.25, the FSP must first notify its customers generally through a public announcement and display prominent notice at the FSP's branches and website. Upon identifying the specific affected customer(s), the FSP must notify the customer(s) directly, as soon as it is feasible to do so.
translation: AI Generated: If the FSP cannot yet identify specific affected customers when notification is required, it must first issue a general public announcement and display notices at branches and its website. Once specific affected customers are identified, they must be notified directly as soon as feasible.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that their staff are trained to handle queries from the affected customers and to provide the necessary assistance on protective measures against any potential consequences that could be caused by the breach.
translation: AI Generated: Staff must be trained to handle queries from affected customers and provide assistance on protective measures against potential consequences of the breach.

verbatim: FSPs must monitor the risks that may arise from outsourced service providers (OSPs) with the functions of the handling of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs are required to actively monitor and manage risks associated with outsourced service providers that handle customer information on their behalf.

verbatim: FSPs must perform adequate and relevant due diligence assessments when selecting an OSP which has access to customer information including for processing, storing, or disposing customer information. These assessments will help FSPs understand the level of risks that may be introduced by the OSP and determine the appropriate monitoring that must be maintained.
translation: AI Generated: Before appointing an OSP with access to customer information, FSPs must conduct thorough due diligence to assess the risks the OSP may introduce, particularly regarding processing, storage, and disposal of customer data, and to determine the level of ongoing monitoring required.

verbatim: FSPs must be satisfied that the OSP has in place policies, procedures and controls that are comparable to that of the FSPs, to ensure that customer information is properly safeguarded at all times.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must verify that their OSPs maintain policies, procedures, and controls at a standard comparable to the FSP's own, ensuring customer information is continuously protected.

verbatim: In ensuring the obligation to safeguard customer information is adequately reflected in a Service Level Agreement (SLA) with an OSP, at a minimum, the SLA must require the OSP to- (a) undertake to safeguard the customer information and prevent any theft, loss, misuse or unauthorised access, modification or disclosure by whatever means, of the customer information; (b) ensure the adequacy and effectiveness of its policies and procedures to protect the FSP's customer information; (c) conduct robust vetting on its personnel who handles customer information; (d) only allow its personnel to have access to customer information strictly for the purpose of carrying out the OSP's functions under the SLA; (e) ensure that its personnel understands and undertakes to comply with the prohibition on disclosure by whatever means of customer information to any person for any purpose other than that which is specified in the SLA, permitted under the written law or approved by BNM, as the case may be (including after the end of the contract term); (f) investigate any customer information breach to determine when and how the breach occurred; (g) report any customer information breach to the FSP within an agreed timeframe; (h) destroy in accordance with paragraph 10.32 or return all customer information to the FSP upon the expiry or termination of the SLA; and (i) allow the FSP to audit or inspect how customer information is safeguarded. [Footnote 24: For clarity, FSPs are expected to amend all of their existing relevant SLAs to comply with paragraph 12.4 and not upon renewal only.]
translation: AI Generated: The SLA with an OSP must, at minimum, obligate the OSP to: (a) safeguard customer data against theft, loss, misuse, or unauthorised access/modification/disclosure; (b) maintain adequate policies and procedures; (c) vet personnel handling customer data; (d) restrict access to SLA purposes only; (e) ensure personnel comply with disclosure prohibitions even after contract ends; (f) investigate any breach; (g) report breaches within agreed timeframes; (h) destroy or return all data upon SLA termination; and (i) permit FSP audits. Existing SLAs must be amended to comply, not just upon renewal.

verbatim: FSPs may provide clear expectations to the OSP on the control measures required in respect of processing, storage, transmission, disposal or destruction of the FSPs' customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs are encouraged to clearly communicate to OSPs the specific control measures expected for processing, storing, transmitting, disposing, or destroying customer information.

verbatim: FSPs must require the OSP to sign a binding non-disclosure undertaking with regard to the handling of customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must ensure that OSPs execute a legally binding non-disclosure agreement covering the handling of customer information.

verbatim: FSPs must ensure that the OSP conducts training to its personnel, at regular intervals, on relevant policies and procedures relating to the proper handling of customer information as well as reviews the adequacy and effectiveness of the training programme.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must ensure OSPs provide regular training to their personnel on customer information handling policies and procedures, and that the effectiveness of the training programme is periodically reviewed.

verbatim: FSPs may consider providing training to the OSPs' personnel to promote awareness of the importance of safeguarding the FSPs' customer information and to ensure compliance with the contractual requirements.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs are encouraged to consider directly training OSP personnel to raise awareness about safeguarding customer information and ensuring compliance with contractual obligations.

verbatim: FSPs must conduct a review of the OSP at least once in every two years to confirm that the OSP fulfils its obligations in accordance with the contract provisions in safeguarding the FSPs' customer information.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must review their OSPs at least biennially to confirm the OSP is meeting its contractual obligations to safeguard customer information.

verbatim: FSPs must take reasonable steps to maintain accurate and complete records and trail of all customer information that have been shared or given to the OSPs.
translation: AI Generated: FSPs must maintain accurate and complete records, including audit trails, of all customer information shared with or provided to OSPs.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
verbatim: A financial institution, its directors and officers must comply with the conditions specified in the table below in relation to permitted disclosures of any customer information as set out under Schedule 11 of the FSA and IFSA as well as the Fourth Schedule of the DFIA.
translation: AI Generated: Financial institutions, including their directors and officers, must adhere to the conditions specified in the accompanying table when making permitted disclosures of customer information under Schedule 11 of the FSA and IFSA, and the Fourth Schedule of the DFIA. The table contains 8 items covering law enforcement compliance, IRBM/tax disclosures, outsourced functions, consultants/adjusters, foreign supervisory authorities, centralised group functions, due diligence exercises, and customer consent.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
purpose: Compliance with an order or request made pursuant to, or requirements imposed under, any written law by any enforcement agency in Malaysia for the purpose of an investigation or prosecution of an offence under any written law.
personsToWhom: An investigating officer duly authorised under any written law to investigate into the commission of an offence under any written law, or any officer duly authorised to carry out prosecution, or any court.
verbatim: Conditions: (a) The request for documents or information must be specific, stating the name or identification number of the customer, the account number, the specific provision of the law under which the request or order is made, and the identity of the officer making the request or order. (b) For written requests, the enforcement agency must use the appropriate form: Appendix II (for requests under any written law other than section 44 of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001 (AMLA)), Appendix III (for requests under section 44 of AMLA), or Appendix IV (for court orders). (c) The written request must be signed by an officer of the appropriate rank: for the Royal Malaysia Police (PDRM), by an officer of the rank of Inspector and above; for the Royal Malaysian Customs Department (Kastam), by the head of division; for all other enforcement agencies, by the most senior ranking officer in the list of officers authorised under the relevant written law. (d) In the case of requests made by PDRM through the electronic Financial Intelligence System Access (eFSA) portal, the following conditions apply: (i) the request must be from a PDRM officer of the rank of Inspector and above; (ii) the request must be made via the eFSA portal administered by BNM; (iii) the financial institution must verify the request through the eFSA portal; and (iv) the financial institution must respond to the request through the eFSA portal. (e) The financial institution must make reasonable enquiries to satisfy itself that the request is properly authorised under the relevant written law. (f) The financial institution must verify the identity and authority of the officer making the request or order. (g) The financial institution must ensure that the enforcement agency is empowered under the relevant written law to take possession of documents or information of the financial institution.
translation: AI Generated: When a law enforcement agency in Malaysia requests customer information for investigation or prosecution, the financial institution must verify that: (1) the request is specific (naming the customer, account, legal provision, and requesting officer); (2) the correct form is used (Appendix II for general requests, Appendix III for AMLA s44, Appendix IV for court orders); (3) the request is signed by an officer of adequate rank (Inspector+ for PDRM, head of division for Kastam, senior authorised officer for others); (4) for PDRM digital requests via eFSA, the request comes from Inspector+ rank, is made and responded to via the eFSA portal, and is verified through it; (5) reasonable enquiries confirm proper authorisation; (6) the officer's identity and authority are verified; and (7) the agency has legal power to obtain such documents.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
purpose: Documents or information required by the Inland Revenue Board of Malaysia (IRBM) under section 81 of the Income Tax Act 1967 (ITA) for the purposes of: (a) exchange of information under section 132, 132A, or 132B of the ITA; or (b) garnishee order under section 106A of the ITA.
personsToWhom: Any officer of the Inland Revenue Board of Malaysia (IRBM) or persons authorised by IRBM.
verbatim: Conditions for exchange of information under section 132, 132A, or 132B of the ITA: (a) The IRBM must provide a written notice identifying the customer whose documents or information are required. (b) The written notice must include a statement that the customer has failed to comply with a notice issued under section 81 of the ITA. (c) The financial institution must notify the customer of the information furnished to IRBM, unless IRBM states that the matter is urgent or that prior notification would undermine the purpose of the request. Conditions for garnishee order under section 106A of the ITA: (a) The IRBM must provide a written notice specifying that it is for the purpose of section 106A of the ITA. (b) The written notice must be signed by the Director of Legal or the Director of Civil and Prosecution Division of IRBM. (c) A copy of the relevant judgement must be attached to the written notice. (d) The financial institution must verify the authenticity of the written notice and accompanying documents if there is any doubt.
translation: AI Generated: For IRBM tax disclosures, two scenarios apply. For exchange of information (s132/132A/132B ITA): IRBM must provide a written notice identifying the customer, state that the customer failed to comply with an s81 notice, and the FI must notify the customer of information furnished unless IRBM indicates urgency or that prior notification would undermine the request. For garnishee orders (s106A ITA): IRBM must provide a written notice for s106A purposes, signed by the Director of Legal or Director of Civil and Prosecution Division, with the relevant judgement attached, and the FI must verify authenticity if in doubt.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
purpose: Performance of functions which are outsourced by the financial institution.
personsToWhom: Any person engaged by the financial institution to perform the outsourced function.
verbatim: Conditions: (a) The financial institution must comply with all outsourcing requirements as specified by BNM. (b) The person engaged to perform the outsourced function must enter into a binding non-disclosure agreement (NDA) with the financial institution, ensuring that the person is bound by confidentiality obligations with respect to any customer information accessed or received in the course of performing the outsourced function.
translation: AI Generated: When outsourcing functions that involve customer information, the financial institution must comply with all BNM outsourcing requirements, and the outsourced service provider must sign a binding NDA ensuring confidentiality obligations over any customer information accessed or received during the outsourced function.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
purpose: Disclosure of customer information to a consultant or adjuster engaged by the financial institution.
personsToWhom: A consultant or adjuster engaged by the financial institution.
verbatim: Conditions: (a) A consultant refers to any person (including an auditor, legal counsel, or any other professional advisor) who is engaged by the financial institution, whether on a temporary or ad hoc basis, to carry out specific tasks for and on behalf of the financial institution. (b) Where a consultant or adjuster is engaged by the head office of a financial institution, the financial institution must be a party to the agreement entered into between the head office and the consultant or adjuster. (c) Customer information may only be disclosed to the consultant or adjuster on a need-to-know basis, limited to information that is reasonably necessary for the consultant or adjuster to carry out the specific tasks for which they are engaged. (d) Access to customer information by the consultant or adjuster must be restricted to the premises of the financial institution in Malaysia, unless the financial institution has obtained the prior written approval of BNM to allow access outside its premises. (e) The consultant or adjuster must enter into a binding non-disclosure agreement (NDA) with the financial institution, ensuring that the consultant or adjuster is bound by confidentiality obligations with respect to any customer information accessed or received. Footnote 28: The condition in item (d) does not apply to summary or collection information that does not identify or allow the identification of individual customers.
translation: AI Generated: For consultants (including auditors, legal counsel, other advisors) or adjusters engaged by the FI: (a) a consultant is defined as any professional engaged on temporary or ad hoc basis for specific tasks; (b) if engaged by head office, the FI must be party to the agreement; (c) disclosure is on need-to-know basis only; (d) access to customer information must be restricted to the FI's premises in Malaysia (unless BNM approval obtained), except for summary/collection information that does not identify individuals (Footnote 28); and (e) a binding NDA must be signed.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
purpose: Performance of supervisory functions by a relevant authority outside Malaysia that corresponds to or is equivalent to Bank Negara Malaysia.
personsToWhom: Any officer of the relevant authority who is duly authorised to receive documents or information.
verbatim: Conditions: (a) The relevant authority must be a foreign supervisory authority exercising supervisory functions for the purpose of group-wide supervision. (b) The request must be made in writing, stating the purpose for which the documents or information are required. (c) Disclosure must not include any deposit account information. (d) BNM must be notified of the disclosure. (e) The financial institution must obtain an undertaking from the relevant authority that the documents or information disclosed will be used solely for supervisory purposes. Note: This item is not applicable to prescribed institutions (per clause 13.2).
translation: AI Generated: For disclosures to foreign supervisory authorities equivalent to BNM for group-wide supervision: (a) the authority must be a foreign supervisor exercising group-wide supervisory functions; (b) the request must be written and state its purpose; (c) deposit account information must not be disclosed; (d) BNM must be notified; and (e) an undertaking must be obtained that information will be used solely for supervisory purposes. This item does not apply to prescribed institutions.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
purpose: Conduct of centralised functions within a financial group, including but not limited to internal audit, risk management, finance and information technology functions.
personsToWhom: The head office or holding company of the financial institution, or any person designated by the head office or holding company to perform centralised functions.
verbatim: Conditions: (a) The centralised functions must be established as part of the regular organisational structure of the financial group and must not be ad hoc or temporary in nature. (b) Customer information may only be disclosed on a need-to-know basis, limited to information that is reasonably necessary for the performance of the centralised functions. (c) The head office or holding company must be regulated by a supervisory authority and must be subject to confidentiality obligations that are at least equivalent to those under the FSA, IFSA, or DFIA. (d) The financial institution must comply with all requirements imposed by BNM in relation to centralised functions. Footnote 29: Where the centralised function is performed by an external party engaged by the head office or holding company, the conditions in item 3 (outsourced functions) or item 4 (consultant/adjuster) shall apply as appropriate. Footnote 30: The conduct of centralised functions is distinct from outsourcing. Centralised functions are performed within the financial group structure, whereas outsourcing involves engaging a third-party service provider outside the group. Note: This item is not applicable to prescribed institutions (per clause 13.2).
translation: AI Generated: For centralised group functions (internal audit, risk management, finance, IT, etc.): (a) these must be established regular organisational functions, not ad hoc; (b) disclosure is on need-to-know basis only; (c) the head office/holding company must be regulated and subject to equivalent confidentiality obligations; and (d) BNM requirements for centralised functions must be complied with. If an external party performs centralised functions, outsourcing (Item 3) or consultant (Item 4) conditions apply (Footnote 29). Centralised functions are distinct from outsourcing as they are within the group structure (Footnote 30). This item does not apply to prescribed institutions.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
purpose: Due diligence exercise approved by the board of directors of the financial institution for the purpose of: (a) a merger and acquisition involving the financial institution; (b) capital raising by the financial institution; or (c) the sale of assets or business of the financial institution.
personsToWhom: Any person participating in the due diligence exercise.
verbatim: Conditions: (a) The disclosure of customer information must be limited to named individuals who are participating in the due diligence exercise, and the disclosure must be time-bound to the duration of the due diligence exercise. (b) All persons to whom customer information is disclosed must enter into a binding non-disclosure agreement (NDA) with the financial institution, ensuring that they are bound by confidentiality obligations with respect to any customer information accessed or received. (c) The disclosure must only take place after: (i) BNM's approval has been obtained under the relevant provisions of the FSA, IFSA, or DFIA for the merger and acquisition, capital raising, or sale of assets or business, as applicable; or (ii) the Minister's approval has been obtained, where required under the FSA, IFSA, or DFIA. Note: This item is not applicable to prescribed institutions (per clause 13.2).
translation: AI Generated: For board-approved due diligence exercises related to M&A, capital raising, or asset/business sales: (a) disclosure is limited to named individuals and time-bound to the due diligence period; (b) all recipients must sign a binding NDA; and (c) disclosure may only occur after BNM or Ministerial approval has been obtained under the relevant statutory provisions. This item does not apply to prescribed institutions.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
purpose: Documents or information which the customer, or the customer's personal representative being the executor, administrator, or legal representative of the customer, has given permission in writing for the disclosure.
personsToWhom: Any person permitted by the customer or the customer's personal representative.
verbatim: Conditions: The consent obtained from the customer must satisfy all four of the following conditions: (a) Specific — The terms of the consent must be drafted in a clear, concise, and plain language manner. The consent must be specific as to: (i) the person or class of persons to whom the disclosure will be made; (ii) the purpose of the disclosure; and (iii) the documents or information or categories thereof that will be disclosed. (b) Voluntary — The financial institution must not compel or coerce the customer into providing consent beyond what is necessary for the products or services requested. The financial institution is prohibited from combining consent for the disclosure of customer information with consent for other unrelated matters in a single statement or form. (c) Explicit and deliberate — The customer must explicitly opt in to provide consent. Silence or inaction on the part of the customer shall not constitute consent. Pre-ticked boxes or default consent mechanisms are prohibited. (d) Revocable upon request — The customer may withdraw consent at any time. The financial institution must inform the customer of the right to withdraw consent. The process for withdrawing consent must be straightforward and must not be more burdensome than the process for giving consent. The financial institution must cease disclosure of the customer's information within seven (7) calendar days from the date of receipt of the customer's withdrawal request. Existing customers may also withdraw consent that was given before the effective date of this policy document. The financial institution must maintain records of consents obtained and withdrawals received, and these records must be readily accessible. Footnote 31: The consent requirements apply to all forms of consent obtained after the effective date of this policy document, including consent obtained through digital channels. Footnote 32: Plain language means language that is easily understood by the average customer, avoiding technical jargon or legal terminology where possible. Footnote 33: The person or class of persons must be described with sufficient specificity to enable the customer to understand who will receive their information. Footnote 34: The purpose must be described with sufficient specificity to enable the customer to understand why their information is being disclosed. Footnote 35: Combining consent for disclosure with consent for other unrelated matters, such as marketing preferences or terms and conditions for products, in a single statement or form is prohibited. Footnote 36: Opt-in mechanisms include, but are not limited to, unticked checkboxes, toggle switches in the off position, or a clear affirmative statement by the customer. Footnote 37: Pre-ticked boxes, pre-selected options, or bundled consent mechanisms that do not allow the customer to separately consent to the disclosure of their information are prohibited. Footnote 38: The seven (7) calendar day period begins from the date the financial institution receives the customer's withdrawal request, regardless of the channel through which the request is made. Footnote 39: The financial institution must maintain a centralised or otherwise accessible record-keeping system that allows it to demonstrate compliance with the consent requirements, including evidence of consent obtained and withdrawal requests processed.
translation: AI Generated: Customer consent-based disclosure (effective 1 January 2024) requires all four conditions to be met: (a) SPECIFIC — consent must be clear, concise, plain language, and specific about who receives the information, the purpose, and what information is disclosed; (b) VOLUNTARY — no compulsion or coercion beyond what is necessary, and consent for disclosure must not be combined with unrelated matters in a single form; (c) EXPLICIT AND DELIBERATE — the customer must actively opt in (no silence, inaction, pre-ticked boxes, or default consent); (d) REVOCABLE — the customer may withdraw at any time, must be informed of this right, the withdrawal process must be no more burdensome than giving consent, the FI must cease disclosure within 7 calendar days of receiving the withdrawal request, existing pre-effective-date consents may also be withdrawn, and the FI must maintain accessible records of all consents and withdrawals.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
verbatim: For the avoidance of doubt, items 5, 6, and 7 in the table below are not applicable to prescribed institutions.
translation: AI Generated: To clarify, items 5 (foreign supervisory authority), 6 (centralised functions within group), and 7 (due diligence for M&A, capital raising, and asset sales) in the permitted disclosure conditions table do not apply to prescribed institutions.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
verbatim: Financial institutions are required to put in place adequate control measures over the disclosure of customer information to any parties which are permitted under the FSA, IFSA or DFIA, which at a minimum shall include- (a) the processes to be undertaken by the officers responsible to verify the authenticity of the orders or requests; (b) documentation requirements; and (c) authority levels for approving disclosure of customer information which must be at an appropriate senior level.
translation: AI Generated: Financial institutions must establish adequate controls over permitted disclosures, including at minimum: (a) processes for responsible officers to verify the authenticity of disclosure orders or requests; (b) documentation requirements; and (c) approval authority levels set at an appropriately senior level.

sectionTitle: Conditions in relation to permitted disclosure
verbatim: Financial institutions intending to apply for BNM's approval for the disclosure of customer information under section 134(1)(b) of the FSA, section 146(1)(b) of the IFSA or section 120(1)(b) of the DFIA must complete and submit the application form in Appendix V to BNM.
translation: AI Generated: Financial institutions seeking BNM approval for customer information disclosure under section 134(1)(b) FSA, section 146(1)(b) IFSA, or section 120(1)(b) DFIA must complete and submit the Appendix V application form to BNM.
